paragraphs.body: Stubborn versus Prevention What if you were lost, never to be found again in the deep foliage of the woods? You push past the warnings of “Don’t go there if you know what’s best for you!”, and “You don’t know what you’re doing”, and keep rustling through the bushes. After brushing aside leaves and stray branches, the hint of a sandy path reaches your eyes. Turns out the only thing that had been holding you back were your own thoughts, and misguided fear. However- what if you weren’t so fortunate- what if you made a simple mistake, or didn’t listen to a certain warning- and became a part of the 600,000 people who become lost annually?

annotations.text: Turns out the only thing that had been holding you back were your own thoughts, and misguided fear.
annotations.comment: heat and friction, here! Let's talk :)

paragraphs.body: While searching for a topic at hand, I happened to come across the YouTube video, “The Horrors of Becoming Lost”, by the channel called Abstract. In the 48-minute-long video, the narrator analyzes through 3 harrowing stories of groups and individuals making different mistakes, which equally led to their demise in nature. Different situations, led to the individuals' making choices that led to their death. With all of this to process, one can’t help but wonder why bother exploring in the first place? I know my mother is for sure one of these people that question exploration. Every time I set off on an excursion her words always linger in my mind- “Why are you going out there all by yourself”, and “I’d rather you just stay on campus!” As the YouTube video, “The Horror of Becoming Lost”, mentions, safety is important, as well as making the right decisions, and sometimes it just requires that extra, unsure step to secure your salvation. But what if you ignored the right directions, and pushed yourself to your own limits?

annotations.text: know my mother is for sure one of these people that question exploration. Every time I set off on an excursion her words always linger in my mind- “Why are you going out there all by yourself”, and “I’d rather you just stay on campus!”
annotations.comment: I wonder if this idea is different from summary and should be developed in its own paragraph? Usually, summary is all "they say" and no "I say."

paragraphs.body: Me and my friend recently went on a hike in Schenley park. The overall hike was 3.7 miles, but the paths were glazed over in slush and ice, and relatively dangerous.
(Our path, dangerously glazed over in hard ice!)

My friend, Julia, excited to explore the forest, eagerly decided we would head off the path we would normally take. I coaxed her out of it, as I kept thinking about the harrowing story video mentioned. The author first told us about Hans, who decided to take a more difficult route he didn't know, leading to the deaths of him and 8 young boys. According to the narrator, “That morning, once he was out the door (and the workers were out of earshot), he simply changed the route. The extent to which the group were aware of exactly what they were getting themselves into is unclear, but they would eventually find themselves in some of the toughest terrain in the area. It's easy to become lost or succumb to a quick change in the weather if you don't know what you're doing and it would seem that Hans didn't.” Similar to Hans, if allowed, Julia would’ve changed her decision hastily. Just like how the quote admits it's easy to become lost based off of a quick decision, we could’ve ended up in an even more dangerous situation, or stranded somewhere had I not reasoned with her to stick to the general path surroundings.

annotations.text: According to the narrator, “That morning, once he was out the door (and the workers were out of earshot), he simply changed the route. The extent to which the group were aware of exactly what they were getting themselves into is unclear, but they would eventually find themselves in some of the toughest terrain in the area. It's easy to become lost or succumb to a quick change in the weather if you don't know what you're doing and it would seem that Hans didn't.”

annotations.context_right: imilar to Hans, if allowed, Jul

paragraphs.body: (We eventually walked to a much safer path with less obstacles, as you can see.)

My gut, listen to my gut. My mom always says this whenever I bring up something that I’m questioning about. When I was younger, I would be annoyed with her saying this, thinking she would just push my feelings aside, but as I grew older, I began to hold this advice dear to my heart. My mom always warns me, and gets nervous whenever I have a urge to go hiking, or caving. My pride would always get in the way, as I’m always the one to reassure her that “I’ve got this”, or “I’ll be alright.” Much before I watched this video, I didn’t really take accord of to most of the danger that lurked within doing these hobbies. Much less, that the very muddy footprints I left in the path could be the last piece of evidence to my very own disappearance.

annotations.text: My gut, listen to my gut. My mom always says this whenever I bring up something that I’m questioning about.

annotations.text: My pride would always get in the way, as I’m always the one to reassure her that “I’ve got this”, or “I’ll be alright.”

paragraphs.body: The author mentions a lot of different ways one can place themselves in a compromising situation. For example- Hans and the 8 boys he led on the what was supposed to be a short trip, ended up dying due to a bad change in plans, and unpredictable weather mixed with a terrain they didn't have knowledge of. Next, the second account in the documentary consists of cave diving. Nuno, Malcom, and Pieter, 3 university students majoring in science, set out to explore a large cave system in South Africa. Nuno did a lot of the diving so he would lead the group, in a 125 meter submerged section. Yet, even with all the knowledge the students thought they had, they still had more things to take into account. The author takes heed to mention, “Even though he was carrying one spare oxygen cylinder, Nuno admits that "by modern standards, we were probably poorly equipped.” Another mistake that could’ve been prevented had Nuno admitted that to the group- unproper preparation. I do admit I’m a chronic over-packer, and even though my mom doesn’t approve of my long hikes, she’s fully aware that I will pack enough food and water to last me as needed. I feel like the author added this line to bring awareness to the importance of making sure to be safe, and fully prepped while beginning an excursion. Continuing on, the group described Peiter as a loner- he would often go ahead by himself, leading to his eventual disappearance. As he was stuck, isolated and losing sanity, he succumbed to starvation. Volunteers found his body and saw that although the state of his oxygen cylinder was scratched from trying to squeeze out the same way he emerged- it was still full.

annotations.comment: Because you keep returning to content and summary of the video, this essay is beginning to feel like reader response rather than an essay based on your _own_ agenda. Let's talk about the audience and purpose of this essay and then work out a "map." (ha. That's sort of an unintentional hiking pun).
annotations.context_left: xygen cylinder was scratched from trying to squeeze out the same way he emerged- it was still full.

paragraphs.body: Leading the question I have, now in this harrowing situation, what choice would you have made? Nuno, the lead explorer in the story quotes, “It's impossible to completely tame the fear which could grow second by second at a relentless pace. Everyone has a breaking point when things start to go wrong. There's no doubt about that. It's only a matter of time until the monster hiding in the big black of the cave will come and get you.’’ The author of the video chose to add, “Pieter had enough oxygen to return but it seems that he chose not to even attempt it. To quote Nuno: "in the fading light he watched the water's surface while calculating if it would be better to dive and never find his way back again or sit in the darkness and wait. He chose the latter." ” It’s humane to feel fear, but sometimes our own mind can get in the way of rational thinking, just as Pieter’s mind prevented him from trying take the plunge to attempt to find a way out. It just makes me think about what his wife thought about his cave diving, and if she warned him not to, just as my mom would’ve warned me to not go! At the end of the day, without risk, there is no reward. At the end of my hike, I encountered a gorgeous sunset. Suddenly, the pain from the walk subsided for just a moment- and I saw why I do these slightly dangerous adventures. I appreciate the beauty of the world, and the small things, way more often.No No matter what, there will always be a light at the end of the tunnel, if you push yourself in the right way forwards. Maybe being a bit stubborn is needed after all.

annotations.text: At the end of the day, without risk, there is no reward.
